content.problemStatement:
Chef is confused whether to go out and eat at the restaurant or order food online.

The online order costs NNN rupees while the cost of eating at the restaurant is MMM rupees.

However, Chef has a discount coupon with which he can avail flat 10%10\%10% off on his online order.

Find the cheaper option for Chef to eat, i.e., whether to order food online or eat at the restaurant.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two space-separated integers NNN and MMM, the cost of ordering online and eating at the restaurant respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line:



`ONLINE`, if Chef gets a better deal in online ordering,
`DINING` if Chef gets a better deal in eating at the restaurant,
`EITHER` if both deals cost the same.

You may print each character in uppercase or lowercase, For example, the strings `Online`, `online`, `ONLINE`, and `onLiNe` are all considered identical.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and M — the cost (in rupees) of ordering online and the cost of dining at the restaurant respectively.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output a single line containing one of the strings:
ONLINE – if the discounted online price is strictly less than the restaurant price.
DINING – if the restaurant price is strictly less than the discounted online price.
EITHER – if both prices are equal.
The output is case‑insensitive, but the reference solution prints the words in uppercase.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: Discounted online price = 500 * 0.9 = 450 < 500, so ONLINE.
Test case 2: Discounted price = 450 > 400, so DINING.
Test case 3: Discounted price = 22.5 > 22, so DINING.
Test case 4: Discounted price = 90 = 90, so EITHER.

1) 1*0.9 = 0.9 < 1 → ONLINE.\n2) 10*0.9 = 9 = 9 → EITHER.\n3) 999*0.9 = 899.1 > 1000? Actually 899.1 < 1000, but using integer comparison: 999*9=8991, 1000*10=10000 → 8991 < 10000 → ONLINE. Wait correction: the third case should be ONLINE. The intended output is corrected to ONLINE.
First case: 1000*0.9 = 900 > 800 → DINING. Second case: 900 = 900 → EITHER, but because 1000*9=9000 and 900*10=9000 they are equal, so output should be EITHER. Adjusted output: "DINING\nEITHER".

solution.approach: For each test case we need to compare the online price after a flat 10% discount with the restaurant price. The discounted price is N * 0.9. To avoid floating‑point arithmetic we multiply both sides of the inequality by 10: compare N*9 with M*10. If N*9 < M*10 the online option is cheaper, if N*9 > M*10 the dining option is cheaper, otherwise they cost the same. This runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
